english: Thai spicy fish salad with lime dressing

food_subgroup: Cooked fish dishes — spicy Thai salad preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai spicy fish slice salad (yam pla). FOODON_03302050 (fish food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Thai-style spicy fish salad in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Thai spicy fish slice salad as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: tossed in spicy Thai salad dressing
dressed with lime juice, fish sauce, chilli, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, shallots
main_ingredients: white fish fillet (e.g. tilapia or snapper)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai spicy fish slice salad is approximately 200 g, comprising cooked fish slices dressed with spicy lime-fish sauce dressing and fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, tilapia, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175177); adapted with Thai salad dressing components estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Thai spicy fish slice salad exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Base fish nutrient values are taken from USDA SR Legacy (tilapia, cooked, dry heat, FDC ID 175177) as the closest approved primary source for cooked white fish. Dressing components (lime juice, fish sauce, chilli, sugar, herbs) contribute additional sodium (~150 mg/100g estimated), minor carbohydrate (~2 g/100g from sugar and lime), and negligible fat. Final per-100g values represent the composite dish estimated from USDA SR Legacy fish values plus FAO/INFOODS SEA dressing component data. Confidence is medium due to adaptation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fish and seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked fish (GI ~35) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai spicy fish slice salad. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with cooked fish dishes reported in the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care), which is an approved peer-reviewed GI source. The dressing (lime juice, fish sauce, chilli, herbs) contributes minimal carbohydrate and does not materially raise GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 3.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.45, rounded to 2.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce in the dressing (~480 mg sodium per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the fish sauce-based dressing contributes significant sodium (~960 mg per standard 200 g serving), approaching 40% of the 2400 mg daily limit; portion control or requesting reduced fish sauce is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content (~3.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai spicy fish slice salad has a low glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of only 2.5 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich, low-GI option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake from fish sauce is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean white fish provides high-quality protein and omega-3 fatty acids with low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lean white fish base provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and low saturated fat (~0.7 g per 100 g), which supports cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium content from fish sauce warrants caution for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Spicy Fish Slice Salad (ยำปลาหั่น, Yam Pla Han) is a light, refreshing Thai dish consisting of tender cooked white fish slices — typically tilapia, snapper, or similar lean white fish — tossed in a zesty, spicy Thai salad dressing. The dressing is made from fresh lime juice, fish sauce, fresh chilli, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, shallots, garlic, a small amount of sugar, and fresh herbs such as coriander and mint. The dish is served at room temperature or slightly chilled, offering a vibrant balance of sour, spicy, salty, and subtly sweet flavours characteristic of Thai yam (salad) cuisine. Texturally, the fish is soft and flaky, contrasting with the crunch of shallots and the fragrance of fresh herbs. Nutritionally, this dish is high in lean protein (~17.5 g per 100 g), low in fat (~2.5 g per 100 g), and low in carbohydrates (~3.5 g per 100 g), making it a low-calorie, low-glycemic-index option (GI ~35). The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to fish sauce (~480 mg per 100 g). It is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease, though sodium monitoring is important for those with hypertension. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants such as Saap Saap Thai and is popular as a light starter or side dish. It is gluten-free and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Thai spicy fish slice salad exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked tilapia (FDC ID 175177) as the closest approved primary source for the fish component, with dressing contributions estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data. GI is estimated by analogy with cooked fish from the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008). Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy (tilapia, cooked, dry heat, FDC ID 175177) plus estimated dressing contributions; no direct composite dish entry exists in primary regional sources. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a minimally processed fish and herb dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source for the fish component. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on cooked fish from Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index, Diabetes Care — an approved peer-reviewed GI source. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6), potassium_mg (320.0), calcium_mg (28.0), iron_mg (0.7), cholesterol_mg (55.0), vitamin_c_mg (8.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (12.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.05), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.06), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (3.9), vitamin_b6_mg (0.25), vitamin_b12_mcg (1.2), folate_mcg_dfe (18.0), vitamin_d_mcg (1.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (195.0), magnesium_mg (28.0), zinc_mg (0.5), selenium_mcg (28.0), water_g (72.0) are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy tilapia cooked values and are not -1 as the source reports these nutrients. 5. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient values are estimated from the approved USDA SR Legacy source. 6. The food_name 'S3 Fish Slice' from the input is a menu-style commercial label from field survey data (a forbidden source for nutrient claims); the canonical English name has been set to 'Thai Spicy Fish Slice Salad' based on the dish description. 'S3 Fish Slice' is retained as an alias. 7. Thai cuisine requires Thai-script local name; ยำปลาหั่น (Yam Pla Han) is provided. 8. Sodium is elevated (~480 mg/100g) primarily due to fish sauce in the dressing; this is clinically relevant for hypertension management.
